preamble.summary: Psalm 7 is a psalm of individual lament and self-imprecation — the psalmist calls on God for refuge from a pursuer, invokes a conditional curse on himself if he is guilty, and appeals to God as righteous judge of all peoples. The psalm describes God as a warrior who prepares weapons against the wicked, then depicts the wicked falling into the very pit they dug. It closes with thanksgiving for God's righteousness.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm contains one of the Psalter's most dramatic oaths of innocence (vv. 4-6, Hebrew vv. 4-6): the psalmist invites his own destruction if he has done what his accuser claims. This is not casual protest but a formal self-imprecation — the ancient equivalent of placing yourself under oath before God's tribunal. The psalm also presents one of the Hebrew Bible's most vivid pictures of self-defeating evil: the wicked dig a pit and fall into it themselves; they conceive trouble and give birth to falsehood. Evil is not merely punished from outside — it devours its own practitioners.
preamble.friction: The superscription identifies this as a shiggayon — a term of uncertain meaning, possibly indicating an emotional, agitated style of composition. The reference to 'Cush the Benjaminite' has no clear parallel in the biblical narratives about David; it may refer to an otherwise unrecorded incident, or 'Cush' may be a coded reference to Saul (also a Benjaminite). The psalm's call for God to 'arise in anger' (v. 7) and judge the nations extends the individual conflict into cosmic scope — the psalmist sees his personal case as a test case for universal justice.
preamble.connections: The self-imprecation formula (vv. 4-6) parallels Job's oath of innocence in Job 31. The pit-and-snare imagery (vv. 16-17) appears in Proverbs 26:27, Ecclesiastes 10:8, and Psalm 9:16. God as righteous judge (shophet tsaddiq) connects to Genesis 18:25 ('Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?'). The warrior-God imagery (vv. 13-14) echoes Deuteronomy 32:41-42.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁגָּי֗וֹן לְדָ֫וִ֥ד אֲשֶׁר־שָׁ֥ר לַיהוָ֑ה עַל־דִּבְרֵי־כ֝֗וּשׁ בֶּן־יְמִינִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Shiggaion of David, which he sang unto the LORD, concerning the words of Cush the Benjamite.
verses.rendering:
A shiggayon of David, which he sang to the LORD
on account of the words of Cush the Benjaminite.
translator_notes: Shiggayon (possibly from shagah, 'to wander, to err, to reel') may indicate a psalm of intense, wandering emotion — an agitated or ecstatic composition. The only other biblical occurrence is in Habakkuk 3:1 (plural: shigionot). The identity of Cush the Benjaminite is unknown. Some scholars connect him to Saul's household (Saul was a Benjaminite), but no narrative in Samuel mentions a figure named Cush. The superscription may preserve a tradition lost to us.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֣ה אֱ֭לֹהַי בְּךָ֣ חָסִ֑יתִי הוֹשִׁיעֵ֥נִי מִכׇּל־רֹ֝דְפַ֗י וְהַצִּילֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD my God, in thee do I put my trust: save me from all them that persecute me, and deliver me:
verses.rendering:
O LORD my God, in You I take refuge;
save me from all who pursue me and rescue me,
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm begins with a confession of refuge and a cry for salvation. YHWH Elohai ('O LORD my God') combines the personal covenant name with the title of divine power. Bekha chasiti ('in You I have taken refuge') uses the perfect tense — the refuge is already claimed, not merely requested. The enemies are rod'fai ('my pursuers, those who chase me') — the language of a hunted person. Two imperatives follow: hoshi'eni ('save me') and hatsileni ('rescue me, deliver me, snatch me away'). The verb natsal means to pull or snatch away from danger, carrying a sense of urgency — to grab someone from the jaws of a predator.
translator_notes: The combination of chasah ('to take refuge') and rod'fai ('my pursuers') creates a vivid scene: the psalmist is being chased and has run to God as a fortress. The imagery is not metaphorical in the abstract — it reflects the ancient practice of seeking sanctuary at a holy place, where the pursued could find protection under divine authority.

verses.text_hebrew: פֶּן־יִטְרֹ֣ף כְּאַרְיֵ֣ה נַפְשִׁ֑י פֹּ֝רֵ֗ק וְאֵ֣ין מַצִּֽיל׃
verses.text_kjv: Lest he tear my soul like a lion, rending it in pieces, while there is none to deliver.
verses.rendering:
lest he tear me apart like a lion,
ripping me to pieces with no one to rescue.
verses.expanded_rendering: The urgency of the petition is explained: pen yitrof ke-aryeh nafshi ('lest he tear my soul like a lion'). The pursuer is compared to a lion — not a distant threat but a predator about to make a kill. The verb taraf ('to tear, to rend, to seize as prey') describes the violence of a predatory attack. Poreq ('rending, tearing apart') intensifies the image — the lion tears the prey into pieces. Ve-ein matsil ('and there is no deliverer, no rescuer') describes the worst scenario: a victim alone with a predator, with no one close enough to intervene. The only hope is God.
translator_notes: Lion imagery for enemies is common in the Psalter (Psalms 10:9, 17:12, 22:14, 22:22, 35:17, 57:5, 58:7). Lions were real threats in ancient Israel (cf. 1 Samuel 17:34, Amos 3:12), and their predatory behavior provided a ready metaphor for enemies who operate by ambush, overwhelming force, and the exploitation of vulnerability.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֣ה אֱ֭לֹהַי אִם־עָשִׂ֣יתִי זֹ֑את אִֽם־יֶשׁ־עָ֥וֶל בְּכַפָּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD my God, if I have done this; if there be iniquity in my hands;
verses.rendering:
O LORD my God, if I have done this —
if there is injustice on my hands —
verses.expanded_rendering: The oath of innocence begins. Im asiti zot ('if I have done this') — the demonstrative 'this' (zot) refers to whatever accusation Cush the Benjaminite has leveled, though the specific charge is unnamed. Im yesh avel be-khappai ('if there is injustice in my hands') — avel ('injustice, wrong, perversity') is examined not in the heart but in the hands, the instruments of action. The Hebrew concept of clean hands (kappayim, 'palms') represents moral innocence demonstrated through conduct (cf. Psalm 24:4, 'clean hands and a pure heart'). The conditional 'if' structure is the formal language of self-imprecation: the psalmist stakes his life on the truth of his innocence.
translator_notes: The self-imprecation (oath with conditional curse) was a legal procedure in the ancient Near East. A person could swear innocence and invoke destruction on themselves if they were lying. It was not undertaken lightly — it placed the swearer under divine judgment. Job 31 is the most extended example in the Hebrew Bible.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־גָּמַ֣לְתִּי שֽׁוֹלְמִ֣י רָ֑ע וָאֲחַלְּצָ֖ה צוֹרְרִ֣י רֵיקָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: If I have rewarded evil unto him that was at peace with me; (yea, I have delivered him that without cause is mine enemy:)
verses.rendering:
if I have repaid my ally with evil
or plundered my enemy without cause —
verses.expanded_rendering: The oath continues with two specific denials. Im gamalti sholmi ra ('if I have repaid the one at peace with me with evil') — sholmi means 'my friend, my ally, one with whom I have a peace covenant.' The accusation would be that the psalmist betrayed a covenant partner — one of the gravest offenses in the ancient world. Va-achaltsah tsorer'ri reqam ('and I stripped my adversary without cause') — even toward an enemy, the psalmist claims to have acted justly. Reqam ('emptily, without cause, for nothing') indicates unprovoked aggression. The oath covers both relationships: the psalmist has neither betrayed friends nor wronged enemies without justification.
translator_notes: The phrase 'or plundered my enemy without cause' is a remarkable ethical claim: even enemies have rights. The psalmist does not claim he has no enemies — he claims he has not wronged them unjustly. This standard exceeds mere loyalty to friends; it requires fairness even toward adversaries.

verses.text_hebrew: יִֽרַדֹּ֥ף אוֹיֵ֨ב ׀ נַפְשִׁ֡י וְיַשֵּׂ֗ג וְיִרְמֹ֣ס לָ֭אָרֶץ חַיָּ֑י וּכְבוֹדִ֓י ׀ לֶעָפָ֖ר יַשְׁכֵּ֣ן סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Let the enemy persecute my soul, and take it; yea, let him tread down my life upon the earth, and lay mine honour in the dust. Selah.
verses.rendering:
then let the enemy pursue me and overtake me;
let him trample my life to the ground
and lay my honor in the dust.
Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The curse upon himself if guilty. Yiraddof oyev nafshi ve-yasseg ('let the enemy pursue my soul and overtake it') — the psalmist invites the very thing he fears most. Ve-yirmos la-arets chayyai ('and let him trample my life to the ground') — the verb ramas ('to trample, to stamp upon') is the language of total defeat, the warrior's foot on the fallen enemy's body. U-khevodi le-afar yashken ('and let my honor be made to dwell in the dust') — kavod ('honor, glory, dignity') reduced to afar ('dust, dirt'). Dust is where the dead go; to have one's honor placed in the dust is to be utterly humiliated and destroyed. The self-imprecation is complete: if I am guilty, let everything I fear come to pass. The gravity of the oath demonstrates the psalmist's confidence in his innocence.
translator_notes: The willingness to invoke one's own destruction is the ultimate act of self-confidence before God's tribunal. The psalmist is so certain of his innocence that he can afford to say: if I am wrong, destroy me. This is the opposite of evasion — it is a direct challenge to God to examine and judge.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֘וּמָ֤ה יְהוָ֨ה ׀ בְּאַפֶּ֗ךָ הִ֭נָּשֵׂא בְּעַבְר֣וֹת צוֹרְרָ֑י וְע֥וּרָה אֵ֝לַ֗י מִשְׁפָּ֥ט צִוִּֽיתָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Arise, O LORD, in thine anger, lift up thyself because of the rage of mine enemies: and awake for me to the judgment that thou hast commanded.
verses.expanded_rendering: Having placed himself under oath, the psalmist now calls on God to judge. Qumah YHWH be-appekha ('rise up, O LORD, in Your anger') echoes the ancient battle cry from Numbers 10:35. Hinnase be-avrot tsorerai ('lift Yourself up against the raging of my adversaries') — the verb nasa ('to lift up, to exalt') calls on God to rise to His full height against the enemy's fury. Urah elai mishpat tsivvita ('awake for me — judgment You have commanded') — the verb ur ('to awake, to rouse') suggests God has been sleeping or inactive (a bold anthropomorphism). The psalmist reminds God that He Himself has ordained justice — mishpat tsivvita ('You commanded judgment'). If God has established the moral order, He is obligated by His own decree to enforce it.
translator_notes: The call for God to 'awake' does not reflect actual belief that God sleeps (Psalm 121:4, 'He who keeps Israel neither slumbers nor sleeps'). It is the language of urgent petition — a sufferer's way of saying: 'Act now, because from my perspective You appear to be doing nothing.' The same bold language appears in Psalm 44:24 and Isaiah 51:9.

verses.text_hebrew: וַעֲדַ֣ת לְ֭אֻמִּים תְּסוֹבְבֶ֑ךָּ וְ֝עָלֶ֗יהָ לַמָּר֥וֹם שֽׁוּבָה׃
verses.text_kjv: So shall the congregation of the people compass thee about: for their sakes therefore return thou on high.
verses.rendering:
Let the assembly of peoples gather around You,
and over them take Your seat on high.
verses.expanded_rendering: The scene expands from personal lawsuit to cosmic courtroom. Adat le'ummim tesovevekka ('the assembly of peoples will surround You') — God is pictured on a judicial throne with the nations gathered as both audience and defendants. Ve-aleha la-marom shuvah ('and above it, to the height, return') — God is asked to take His elevated position over the assembly, the supreme judge presiding from on high. The psalmist's private case has become a public hearing before all nations. This expansion reflects the Psalter's conviction that individual justice and cosmic justice are connected — when God judges rightly in one case, He establishes justice for all.
translator_notes: The image of God enthroned above a gathered assembly of peoples combines courtroom and throne room. The nations serve as witnesses to God's just judgment. This scene anticipates the cosmic judgment imagery in Psalms 96-99, where God judges the peoples with equity.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֥ה יָדִ֗ין עַ֫מִּ֥ים שׇׁפְטֵ֥נִי יְהוָ֑ה כְּצִדְקִ֖י וּכְתֻמִּ֣י עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD shall judge the people: judge me, O LORD, according to my righteousness, and according to mine integrity that is in me.
verses.rendering:
The LORD judges the peoples.
Vindicate me, O LORD,
according to my righteousness and my integrity.
verses.expanded_rendering: The universal principle — YHWH yadin ammim ('the LORD judges the peoples') — grounds the personal petition: shofteni YHWH ('judge me, O LORD'). The psalmist wants to be judged, not exempted from judgment. The standard he offers: ke-tsidqi u-ke-tummi alai ('according to my righteousness and according to my integrity that is upon me'). Tsidqi ('my righteousness') is his conformity to God's moral order. Tummi ('my integrity, my wholeness') from tam (the same word used to describe Job in Job 1:1) describes moral completeness — not perfection but consistency, a life without double dealing. The psalmist does not claim sinlessness but claims that in the specific case at hand, his hands are clean and his cause is just.
translator_notes: The willingness to be judged 'according to my righteousness' is not arrogance but legal confidence in a specific case. The psalmist is not claiming universal sinlessness (the penitential psalms prove otherwise) but innocence regarding the particular accusation. Context determines whether 'judge me according to my righteousness' is presumptuous or appropriate.

verses.text_hebrew: יִגְמׇר־נָ֬א רַ֨ע ׀ רְשָׁעִים֮ וּתְכוֹנֵ֢ן צַ֫דִּ֥יק וּבֹחֵ֣ן לִ֭בּ֥וֹת וּכְלָי֗וֹת אֱלֹהִ֥ים צַדִּֽיק׃
verses.text_kjv: Oh let the wickedness of the wicked come to an end; but establish the just: for the righteous God trieth the hearts and reins.
verses.rendering:
Let the evil of the wicked come to an end,
but establish the righteous —
You who test hearts and minds, O righteous God.
verses.expanded_rendering: The prayer now asks for definitive resolution. Yigmor na ra resha'im ('let the evil of the wicked cease, come to an end') — the verb gamar ('to complete, to finish, to bring to an end') asks for a full stop to wickedness. U-tekhonen tsaddiq ('but establish the righteous') — the verb kun ('to establish, to make firm, to secure') asks God to give the righteous a stable, unshakeable foundation. The basis for this double request: vochen libboth u-khelayot Elohim tsaddiq ('the one who tests hearts and kidneys is God, the righteous one'). The 'kidneys' (kelayot) in Hebrew thought are the seat of deep emotion and secret motive — the organs that hold what the person shows to no one. God examines both libboth ('hearts,' the seat of thought and will) and kelayot ('kidneys,' the seat of hidden feeling). Nothing is concealed from this judge. The epithet Elohim tsaddiq ('righteous God') closes the verse: God's own righteousness is the guarantee that His examination will be fair.
translator_notes: The 'hearts and kidneys' pairing (lev and kelayot) represents the totality of the inner person. The heart thinks; the kidneys feel. Together they encompass the full range of human interiority. God's testing of both means that judgment is based on complete knowledge — motive, intention, and emotion, not just outward action. Jeremiah 11:20 and 17:10 use the identical phrase.

verses.text_kjv: My defence is of God, which saveth the upright in heart.
verses.rendering:
My shield is with God,
who saves the upright in heart.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist states his defense: maginni al Elohim ('my shield rests upon God, my protection is from God'). The magen ('shield') is placed al ('upon, with, from') God — the defense originates in God, not in human strategy. The characterization of God follows: moshia yishre lev ('the one who saves the upright in heart'). The yishre lev ('straight of heart, upright in heart') are people whose interior matches their exterior — no hidden agenda, no concealed corruption. God saves those whose hearts are aligned. The verse functions as a bridge: the psalmist has appealed to God as judge (vv. 7-10) and now affirms that this judge is also a savior — specifically, a savior of those whose hearts are right.
translator_notes: The phrase yishre lev ('upright in heart') echoes the yashar ('upright') of Job 1:1 and the derekh ('way') theology of Psalm 1. Uprightness of heart — internal moral alignment — is the consistent prerequisite for divine protection throughout the wisdom tradition.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֥ים שׁוֹפֵ֥ט צַדִּ֑יק וְ֝אֵ֗ל זֹעֵ֥ם בְּכׇל־יֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: God judgeth the righteous, and God is angry with the wicked every day.
verses.rendering:
God is a righteous judge,
a God who shows indignation every day.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm declares God's judicial character: Elohim shophet tsaddiq ('God is a righteous judge'). The word shophet ('judge') in Hebrew means not merely a courtroom official but a ruler who sets things right — judges deliver, vindicate, and restore order (cf. the book of Judges). Ve-El zo'em be-khol yom ('and God shows indignation every day') — the verb za'am ('to be indignant, to denounce, to rage') describes God's daily response to injustice. This is not occasional wrath triggered by exceptional evil but sustained moral indignation — God's baseline reaction to a world saturated with wrong. Justice is not a periodic intervention but God's continuous activity.
translator_notes: The phrase be-khol yom ('every day') modifying God's indignation is remarkable. It transforms divine anger from a sudden eruption into a steady state — God is perpetually opposed to evil. This daily indignation is the dark side of daily faithfulness: the God who is faithful every morning (Lamentations 3:23) is also indignant every day.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־לֹ֣א יָ֭שׁוּב חַרְבּ֣וֹ יִלְט֑וֹשׁ קַשְׁתּ֥וֹ דָ֝רַ֗ךְ וַֽיְכוֹנְנֶֽהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: If he turn not, he will whet his sword; he hath bent his bow, and made it ready.
verses.rendering:
If one does not repent, God sharpens His sword;
He bends His bow and makes it ready.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm now describes God as a warrior preparing for combat against the unrepentant. Im lo yashuv ('if he does not turn back, repent, return') — the conditional gives the wicked one more chance. But if there is no teshuvah ('repentance, turning'), then: charbo yiltosh ('He sharpens His sword') — latash means to sharpen, to whet, to hone a blade to its keenest edge. Qashto darakh ('He bends His bow, He treads on His bow') — the ancient composite bow required stepping on it to string it, an image of deliberate preparation. Va-yekhoneneha ('and He prepares it, makes it ready') — the weapon is not merely available but actively prepared for use. The imagery is of measured, deliberate preparation — God does not strike impulsively but readies judgment with precision.
translator_notes: The warrior-God imagery appears throughout the Hebrew Bible (Exodus 15:3, Deuteronomy 32:41-42, Isaiah 42:13). The image of God sharpening a sword and bending a bow is not primitive theology but vivid metaphor for divine justice that acts forcefully against entrenched evil. The conditional clause ('if he does not repent') preserves the possibility of mercy up to the last moment.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭לוֹ הֵכִ֣ין כְּלֵי־מָ֑וֶת חִ֝צָּ֗יו לְדֹלְקִ֥ים יִפְעָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath also prepared for him the instruments of death; he ordaineth his arrows against the persecutors.
verses.rendering:
He has prepared deadly weapons against him;
He makes His arrows into flaming shafts.
verses.expanded_rendering: The armament intensifies. Ve-lo hekhin kele mavet ('and for him He has prepared instruments of death') — kele mavet ('weapons of death, deadly instruments') are the full arsenal of divine judgment. Chitsav le-dolqim yif'al ('His arrows He makes for burning, for the pursuers') — the arrows are transformed into dolqim ('burning ones, flaming shafts'). Fire-tipped arrows were used in ancient warfare for maximum destruction. The image of God as archer, firing burning arrows at the wicked, combines the precision of archery with the consuming power of fire. Every weapon is directed le-dolqim — at those who 'burn' or 'pursue hotly,' the relentless persecutors of the innocent.
translator_notes: The word dolqim is ambiguous: it may mean 'burning' (describing the arrows as fiery) or 'hot pursuers' (describing the targets as relentless persecutors). The ambiguity may be intentional — fire meets fire; the burning arrows are aimed at those who burn with malice.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֗ה יְחַבֶּל־אָ֥וֶן וְהָרָ֥ה עָמָ֑ל וְ֝יָלַ֗ד שָֽׁקֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, he travaileth with iniquity, and hath conceived mischief, and brought forth falsehood.
verses.rendering:
See — he conceives iniquity,
is pregnant with trouble,
and gives birth to falsehood.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm shifts from God's weapons to the wicked person's self-destruction, using a birth metaphor in reverse order: yechabbal aven ('he conceives, labors with iniquity'), harah amal ('is pregnant with trouble, mischief'), yalad shaqer ('and gives birth to falsehood, deception'). The three stages — conception, pregnancy, birth — are applied to evil's gestation inside the wicked person. Iniquity is conceived in the mind, carried as trouble in the life, and delivered as falsehood into the world. The metaphor reveals evil as an organic process: it grows inside the one who harbors it, and what it produces is shaqer ('falsehood, deception, disappointment'). The wicked person is both parent and victim of his own evil — he carries it to term and what he births destroys him.
translator_notes: The birth metaphor for evil appears in James 1:15 ('sin, when it is fully grown, gives birth to death'), suggesting continuity between this psalm's imagery and New Testament moral theology. The point is identical: evil is not static but dynamic — it grows, develops, and produces offspring worse than itself.

verses.text_hebrew: בּ֣וֹר כָּ֭רָה וַֽיַּחְפְּרֵ֑הוּ וַ֝יִּפֹּ֗ל בְּשַׁ֣חַת יִפְעָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: He made a pit, and digged it, and is fallen into the ditch which he made.
verses.rendering:
He digs a pit and hollows it out,
then falls into the hole he has made.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's most iconic image: bor karah va-yachperehu ('he digs a pit and excavates it') — two verbs of digging emphasize the deliberateness of the trap. This is not accidental evil but engineered destruction — the wicked person invests labor in creating a trap for the righteous. Va-yippol be-shachat yif'al ('and he falls into the pit he made') — the maker of the trap becomes its victim. The pit (bor) intended for the righteous receives the wicked. This is poetic justice in its purest form — not imposed from outside but inherent in the structure of evil itself. The moral order is self-correcting: schemes aimed at others curve back toward their source. The principle appears across wisdom literature: evil is not merely punished but self-defeating.
translator_notes: The pit-digging image is one of the most widespread motifs in ancient Near Eastern wisdom. It appears in Proverbs 26:27 ('whoever digs a pit will fall into it'), Ecclesiastes 10:8 ('he who digs a pit will fall into it'), and Psalm 9:16 ('the nations sink into the pit they made'). The consistency of the image across traditions reflects a deeply held conviction that the moral order is structured for boomerang justice.

verses.text_hebrew: יָשׁ֣וּב עֲמָל֣וֹ בְרֹאשׁ֑וֹ וְעַ֥ל קׇ֝דְקֳד֗וֹ חֲמָס֥וֹ יֵרֵֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: His mischief shall return upon his own head, and his violent dealing shall come down upon his own pate.
verses.rendering:
His trouble returns on his own head;
his violence comes down on his own skull.
verses.expanded_rendering: The principle from verse 16 is stated explicitly. Yashuv amalo ve-rosho ('his trouble returns upon his own head') — the verb shuv ('to return, to come back') makes the boomerang explicit: the mischief (amal, 'trouble, toil, harm') does not stay where it was aimed but comes back to the one who launched it. Ve-al qodqodo chamaso yered ('and upon his skull his violence descends') — the qodqod ('crown of the head, skull') is the most vulnerable point on the body, and chamas ('violence, wrongdoing, cruelty') lands precisely there. The wicked person is struck by his own weapons, crushed by his own violence. The moral architecture of the universe ensures return-to-sender justice.
translator_notes: The word chamas ('violence') is one of the Hebrew Bible's most theologically loaded terms. It was the reason for the flood (Genesis 6:11, 'the earth was filled with chamas') and one of the prophets' most frequent accusations (Habakkuk 1:2-3, Amos 3:10). When chamas returns on the perpetrator's head, cosmic justice is being served.

verses.text_hebrew: אוֹדֶ֣ה יְהוָ֣ה כְּצִדְק֑וֹ וַ֝אֲזַמְּרָ֗ה שֵׁם־יְהוָ֥ה עֶלְיֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: I will praise the LORD according to his righteousness: and will sing praise to the name of the LORD most high.
verses.rendering:
I will give thanks to the LORD for His righteousness
and sing praise to the name of the LORD Most High.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm that began with a cry for refuge ends with thanksgiving. Odeh YHWH ke-tsidqo ('I will give thanks to the LORD according to His righteousness') — the thanks are proportional to God's tsedaqah ('righteousness, justice, right action'). The psalmist praises not a generic attribute but what God has specifically done: acted righteously in this case. Va-azammerah shem YHWH Elyon ('and I will sing to the name of the LORD Most High') — the verb zamar ('to sing, to make music, to praise with instruments') is the root of mizmor ('psalm'). The title Elyon ('Most High') places YHWH above all other powers — the supreme judge whose courtroom the nations fill, whose arrows the wicked fear, and whose righteousness the faithful celebrate. The psalm moves from terror to song, from pursued victim to praising worshiper.
translator_notes: The title Elyon ('Most High') is one of God's most ancient names, appearing in Genesis 14:18-22 (Melchizedek, priest of El Elyon). It emphasizes God's supreme sovereignty over all competing powers — political, spiritual, and cosmic. Combined with YHWH, it affirms that the covenant God of Israel is also the supreme ruler of the universe.
